nativeRange.habitat: Sand and mud flats in coastal shallows; burrowers that lie buried in soft substrate with a long siphon extended above the surface, emerging at the smell of protein.

decorPreferences: mature reef tank with detritus to scavenge

diet.notes: Carnivorous scavenger of detritus, uneaten meaty foods, and carrion. Will spring out of the sand within seconds of any protein hitting the substrate. In low-bioload tanks supplemental feeding with sinking pellets, frozen mysis, or pieces of fish or scallop is generally advised. Does not eat algae — this is not an algae-grazing snail.

avoidWith: larger hermit crabs that may evict them for shells

summary: A small sand-bed scavenger that springs out of the substrate at the scent of protein. Generally considered the standard detritivore mollusc for reef-tank clean-up crews.
careNotes: Nassarius Snail is an umbrella name for several closely related Nassariidae sold to the reef trade — most commonly Nassarius vibex from the Caribbean/Western Atlantic and Nassarius distortus (the 'Super Tongan' variant) from the Indo-Pacific, with assorted Nassarius spp. mixed in. All share essentially the same husbandry. They are obligate sand-bed burrowers: a sand substrate of at least roughly 2.5 cm is essential, and bare-bottom tanks should be avoided. By day they bury, leaving a long tube-like siphon protruding above the substrate to sample the water for food. When meaty food is detected the substrate appears to 'boil' as snails erupt and swarm the food. They are generally considered reef-safe and are widely used to prevent sand-bed compaction and aerate the upper substrate. They are strictly carnivorous scavengers — they do not eat algae, so they are commonly considered a complement to grazing snails (Trochus, Cerith, Astrea) rather than a substitute. In low-bioload tanks supplemental sinking pellets or frozen mysis are advised; snails seen climbing the glass have been reported as starving. Like other invertebrates they are copper-sensitive and intolerant of medications dosed at therapeutic copper levels. They have been reported to live around 2-3 years in well-kept aquariums and are extremely difficult to breed in captivity (larvae require 1-2 months of planktonic development). Source sizes spanned 1-2 cm (Aquarium Breeder) to 0.5-1 inch / ~1.3-2.5 cm (LiveAquaria, ReefBay); written as a 1-2.5 cm range to capture both. Salinity sources unanimously gave SG 1.023-1.025.
breedingNotes: Extremely difficult to breed in captivity. Larvae are planktonic and have been reported to require 1-2 months of free-swimming development before settling, which is rarely achieved in a home aquarium. Hobbyist stock is overwhelmingly wild-collected from Caribbean and Indo-Pacific shallows.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Species Spotlight - Nassarius sp.
additional.notes: Confirmed parameters (SG 1.023-1.025, 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12) and trade-grouping of Nassarius distortus (Super Tongan) under the Nassarius sp. umbrella.

additional.name: ReefBay - Complete Nassarius Snail Care Guide
additional.notes: Designated Reef2Reef Wiki secondary returned 403; ReefBay used as fallback secondary. Cross-checked size (0.5-1 in), lifespan (2-3 yr), reef-safety (yes), sand-bed requirement (1-2 in / 2.5-5 cm), and trade species (N. vibex, N. distortus). Confirmed strictly carnivorous (does not eat algae).

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder - Nassarius Snail Care
additional.notes: Third source for size (1-2 cm), parameter ranges, lifespan (up to 3 yr), and breeding-difficulty notes (planktonic larvae 1-2 months).

additional.name: Wikipedia - Nassarius
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Nassariidae, Neogastropoda) and worldwide distribution on mud/sand flats intertidally to subtidally.
